steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks for a quotation that illustrates Aristophanes' claim that sophists' practices and views are foolish. To illustrate means to show through a direct example — the quotation must come from a sophist's own words and make the foolishness plain on its face, not merely describe them or report secondhand that they are foolish.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "Strepsiades, after taking lessons from a sophist, says to his son, "Approach, that you may know more; and I will tell you a thing, by learning which you will be a man. But see that you do not teach this to any one."": Strepsiades is a student of sophists, not a sophist himself. His boastful secrecy about the lesson is more comic than it is a direct illustration of sophists' foolish views. ✗
  • "Socrates, a sophist, says to a potential customer, "I wish to briefly learn from you if you are possessed of a good memory."": Socrates is a sophist, but this line is a neutral intake question with no foolish reasoning on display. The foolishness of his views is not illustrated. ✗
  • "Strepsiades encourages his son to learn to be a sophist, saying, "If you have any concern for your father's patrimony, become one of them."": Again, Strepsiades is the student, not a sophist. His financial motivation for pushing his son toward sophistry does not show a sophist's own foolish thinking. ✗
  • "Socrates, a sophist, explains why he studies astronomy while sitting in a basket hanging a few feet off the ground, saying, "I should not have rightly discovered things celestial if I had not suspended the intellect, and mixed the thought in a subtle form with its kindred air."": Socrates is a sophist, and his explanation — that elevating himself physically allows his intellect to "mix with its kindred air" — is self-evidently absurd reasoning. A sophist's own words expose his foolish thinking directly. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
